title: The Acupuncturist

jokes.text: If you can fix my neck, I'll give you $5,000. And if you can't, then I don't pay for any of the treatments.
jokes.setup: Larry's cynicism about medical treatments sets up his confidence in making this bet
jokes.punchline: The absurdly high stakes wager shows Larry's impulsiveness and confidence
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry impulsiveness - turns a routine medical visit into high-stakes gambling. Character-specific and sets up episode's central conflict.

jokes.text: Well, it would be like taking candy from a baby, but...
jokes.setup: Acupuncturist should be confident in his abilities
jokes.punchline: His casual dismissiveness reveals overconfidence that will backfire
jokes.explanation: Standard overconfidence setup, but economically delivered and fits the character dynamic.

jokes.text: It's not in my family, but I'm trying to break the mold.
jokes.setup: Acupuncturist mentions honor is a family tradition
jokes.punchline: Larry acknowledges his family lacks honor but he's attempting to be honorable
jokes.explanation: Self-deprecating humor that's perfectly Larry - acknowledging character flaws while claiming to transcend them. Economical and quotable.

jokes.text: No, I mean, I'm just shocked to see you here working.
jokes.setup: Larry encounters former SNL writer Barry working in a deli
jokes.punchline: Larry's blunt shock at Barry's fall from grace creates awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry foot-in-mouth moment. The cringe comes from his inability to filter his surprise appropriately.

jokes.text: Try and put that on the swiss if you can. I covered my mouth.
jokes.setup: Barry is telling emotional story about his dying father
jokes.punchline: Larry interrupts with practical deli counter hygiene concerns
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed interruption that shows Larry's inability to prioritize emotional moments over practical concerns. The juxtaposition is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: Boy, he's got seven total bases there, yeah.
jokes.setup: Barry mentions father had triple and quadruple bypass surgeries
jokes.punchline: Larry reduces serious medical procedures to baseball terminology
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that deflects from the seriousness of the situation, typical Larry defense mechanism.

jokes.text: It's amazing that you came in here today. It could really help me out. I need to borrow $5,000.
jokes.setup: Barry acts like Larry's arrival is serendipitous
jokes.punchline: Reveals he just wants to borrow money, making the 'amazing coincidence' manipulative
jokes.explanation: Well-structured reveal that shows Barry's manipulation. The timing of the ask right after the sob story is perfectly cynical.

jokes.text: Of course you do. Yeah, of course.
jokes.setup: Barry asks for $5,000 loan
jokes.punchline: Larry's weary recognition that of course Barry wants money
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery that shows Larry's experience with people asking him for money. The repetition adds emphasis.

jokes.text: He's not an asshole, he's just shy. No, he doesn't, he's just shy. It's just that you got shy-asshole confusion, my friend.
jokes.setup: Jeff calls Barry an asshole
jokes.punchline: Larry invents 'shy-asshole confusion' as a medical condition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant creation of a fake psychological condition. The parallel structure and medical terminology make it feel authoritative and absurd.

jokes.text: You know why? 'Cause you're a pussy. I'm just being frank. Maybe I'm nice. You have nice-pussy confusion.
jokes.setup: Jeff calls Larry a pussy for not saying no to requests
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately applies his 'confusion' formula to Jeff's diagnosis
jokes.explanation: Excellent immediate callback that shows Larry applying his newly invented concept. The speed of the callback and parallel structure work perfectly.

jokes.text: Are those from your mistress and you haven't told me? Yeah, I wish.
jokes.setup: Cheryl jokingly asks if flowers are from Larry's mistress
jokes.punchline: Larry's honest wish he had a mistress to send him flowers
jokes.explanation: Larry's characteristic honesty about inappropriate desires. Economical but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: Did you give him a $5,000 tip?
jokes.setup: Flower delivery arrives
jokes.punchline: Cheryl's assumption that Larry's excessive tipping caused the flowers
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Cheryl knows Larry's tendency to overtip, but not exceptional craft.

jokes.text: I think it's some sort of Japanese gay thing.
jokes.setup: Larry asks Jeff about the orchids from acupuncturist
jokes.punchline: Jeff's blunt, politically incorrect assessment
jokes.explanation: Relies on casual racism/homophobia for humor, which feels cheap rather than clever.

jokes.text: I knew you'd open up a can of worms just by starting to ask him. He's been weird all lunch. What the hell? I don't know what that was. That guy's a freak.
jokes.setup: Larry asks Japanese businessman about orchid customs
jokes.punchline: The man's explosive angry reaction to the question
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy moment with Larry inadvertently triggering someone. The aftermath reactions sell the absurdity.

jokes.text: You're encroaching in my territory. I want you people to be uncomfortable all the time.
jokes.setup: Barry's wearing casual clothes on Casual Friday
jokes.punchline: Larry's possessive attitude toward being the only casually dressed person
jokes.explanation: Great Larry logic - he wants to maintain his identity as the slob while everyone else stays formal. Character-specific thinking.

jokes.text: If you don't like this, you should come by on Naked Tuesdays.
jokes.setup: Larry complains about Casual Friday
jokes.punchline: Barry escalates to an even more absurd dress code day
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke that pushes the dress code concept to absurdity. Economical delivery.

jokes.text: I'm telling you, soon it's going to be Casual Monday. Five to 10 years. That's the direction we're heading. It's gonna be a sad state of affairs.
jokes.setup: Larry's upset about Casual Friday
jokes.punchline: His apocalyptic vision of workplace dress codes deteriorating
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry catastrophizing - turning minor social change into societal collapse. The specific timeline makes it feel prophetic.

jokes.text: People like you walking around like people like me. It's no good.
jokes.setup: Larry's concern about dress code deterioration
jokes.punchline: His horror at normal people looking like him
jokes.explanation: Perfect self-aware moment where Larry recognizes his own slovenliness and wants to maintain the class distinction. Economical and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Jesus!
jokes.setup: They're at restaurant, presumably getting served flambé
jokes.punchline: Their shocked reaction to something going wrong with the flambé
jokes.explanation: Generic reaction to physical comedy. Without seeing the visual, relies on assumption of slapstick.

jokes.text: They're not jeans, they're corduroy pants. That's the same thing.
jokes.setup: Cheryl tells Larry he can't wear jeans to the party
jokes.punchline: Larry's distinction without a difference argument
jokes.explanation: Typical marital argument where Larry makes a technical distinction that misses the point. Relatable dynamic.

jokes.text: Since when is blue and black no good?
jokes.setup: Cheryl rejects Larry's color combination
jokes.punchline: Larry's genuine confusion about basic fashion rules
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry obliviousness to social conventions. His genuine confusion is endearing and relatable.

jokes.text: Maybe I have a lime-green T-shirt like yours to put on, asshole. What's wrong with a lime-green T-shirt?
jokes.setup: Larry's forced to change clothes
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic suggestion followed by Jeff's defensive response about his own shirt
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's passive-aggression and Jeff's obliviousness to his own fashion choices.

jokes.text: That's why we got you a birthday gift. 'Cause he told me it was your birthday. So, I knew I would stop by for the sunglasses and I....
jokes.setup: They arrive at surprise party but ruin it by encountering birthday girl first
jokes.punchline: Larry's awkward attempt to explain why they have a gift
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe moment where Larry's attempt to cover makes things worse. The trail-off shows his realization he's digging deeper.

jokes.text: You are so selfish.
jokes.setup: They've ruined the surprise party
jokes.punchline: Carol's blunt assessment of Larry's selfishness
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character reaction. The economy of the insult makes it land harder.

jokes.text: Larry had to change his outfit and it was just a tremendous amount of time. He had to eat, you know, have a big dinner for two hours.
jokes.setup: Explanation for being late to surprise party
jokes.punchline: Cheryl's increasingly absurd list of Larry's selfish delays
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how Larry's selfishness compounds. Cheryl's exasperation comes through clearly.

jokes.text: Sit down, have a big fucking meal in the hotel.
jokes.setup: Cheryl's explanation of Larry's delays
jokes.punchline: Barry's furious continuation of the selfish behavior list
jokes.explanation: Continues the escalation effectively with Barry's anger adding fuel to the list of Larry's selfishness.

jokes.text: I mean, 600,000 people. 600,000 people died in the Civil War. And for what, to save the Union? Why did we want to save the Union? Who cares?
jokes.setup: Party conversation about Civil War
jokes.punchline: Larry's casual dismissal of the importance of the Civil War
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Larry to question fundamental historical assumptions with casual nihilism. The repetition of statistics adds emphasis.

jokes.text: I mean, I wish my worthless son could get into something like that, but no, there's no chance of that.
jokes.setup: Discussion of environmentalist's work
jokes.punchline: Father's brutal assessment of his own son Barry
jokes.explanation: Dark humor from a father's complete lack of faith in his son. Sets up the family dynamic effectively.

jokes.text: Do you get that? Outfit approval? They got to approve your outfit. I listened to her. I actually went up to change.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about having to change clothes
jokes.punchline: His incredulity at the concept of outfit approval while admitting he complied
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the earlier outfit conflict. Larry's indignation while admitting compliance shows his character contradiction.

jokes.text: Let's see. It might stand for 'certified deposit'. For the NRDC.
jokes.setup: Asked what C.D. stands for
jokes.punchline: Implies his name/initials are about making deposits to environmental group
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that also serves the plot by showing his interest in donating to the environmental cause.

jokes.text: I don't know how that schlep ever married you. Me neither.
jokes.setup: C.D. insults Barry to Cheryl
jokes.punchline: Cheryl's immediate agreement about her husband being a schlep
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective setup-punchline with Cheryl's instant agreement surprising both the old man and the audience.

jokes.text: They might take it up the ass for all I know in Japan. What are you asking me for? It's a stupid question.
jokes.setup: Larry asks about Japanese orchid customs
jokes.punchline: C.D.'s crude and hostile response to the innocent question
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor from the old man's inappropriate response, but relies on crude language rather than clever construction.

jokes.text: And I'll tell you something. I'm lucky that I had a father who was so supportive of me, because without him it never would have happened.
jokes.setup: Larry talking to Barry's father about success
jokes.punchline: Sets up the contrast with Barry's unsupportive father sitting right there
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the story that will change the father's mind. The irony works because the audience knows Barry's situation.

jokes.text: Next thing I knew, he said to me, 'Listen, I got a lot of money. I'm gonna give you this money anyway, after I die. Let me give you some now.'
jokes.setup: Larry's story about his supportive father
jokes.punchline: The exact opposite approach from what C.D. is taking with Barry
jokes.explanation: Perfectly constructed to make C.D. realize his mistake. The logic is unassailable and the timing is perfect for maximum impact.

jokes.text: Listen, put a sock in it. I got to move fast. Do you know of an estate lawyer?
jokes.setup: Larry's story about supportive father
jokes.punchline: C.D.'s abrupt interruption showing he's completely changed his mind
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff showing the immediate impact of Larry's story. The urgency and rudeness are perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Your little conspiracy with Cheryl worked. He cut me out of the will, Larry.
jokes.setup: Barry confronts Larry about father changing will
jokes.punchline: Reveals Larry's good deed completely backfired
jokes.explanation: Perfect Curb escalation where Larry's attempt to help creates a worse disaster. The conspiracy theory adds paranoid comedy.

jokes.text: What's this outfit? Is it a Halloween party, or what? I know, it's Casual Friday. Casual Friday? What does that mean?
jokes.setup: C.D. meets estate lawyer on Casual Friday
jokes.punchline: Same reaction Larry had to casual dress in professional setting
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Larry's earlier Casual Friday rant, showing that other people share his conservative dress code views.

jokes.text: You look like a fucking cowboy. You're J.R., Joel Reynolds, right? Look, we-- You belong in Dallas, not LA.
jokes.setup: C.D. angry at lawyer's casual dress
jokes.punchline: Connects lawyer's name to Dallas TV show character J.R. Ewing
jokes.explanation: Clever pop culture reference that works with the cowboy appearance. The J.R./Joel Reynolds connection feels earned rather than forced.

jokes.text: Why, you shit....
jokes.setup: Larry tries to apologize and explain at restaurant
jokes.punchline: C.D.'s shocked recognition leads to him throwing water
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy escalation that sets up the death, but the comedy is more about the setup than the moment itself.

jokes.text: Larry was the last person to see him alive. Technically, it was the waiter.
jokes.setup: Barry says Larry was last person to see father alive
jokes.punchline: Larry's technical correction about the waiter
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry moment - making pedantic corrections even at a funeral. The word 'technically' is perfectly chosen and the timing is impeccable.

jokes.text: My best friend had a baby 15 years ago, and I was the godfather. I was in the delivery room, he asked me to cut the umbilical cord and I don't know, I kind of botched it somehow and mangled the whole bellybutton and now the kid won't even talk to me.
jokes.setup: Larry telling story to button salesman at funeral
jokes.punchline: His complete incompetence at a simple task ruined his relationship with child
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd story that's completely believable for Larry. The escalation from simple task to lifelong estrangement is perfect.

jokes.text: I told you you'd get it as soon as he died, and you made it happen. You kind of did.
jokes.setup: Barry gives Larry the $5,000 check
jokes.punchline: Implies Larry killed his father to get the money back
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy suggesting Larry committed murder for financial gain. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You're a bounty hunter. You murdered my uncle for $5,000.
jokes.setup: Doug learns about the money arrangement
jokes.punchline: He immediately accuses Larry of being a professional killer
jokes.explanation: Great escalation to absurdity. The bounty hunter accusation is perfectly ridiculous.

jokes.text: You took $5,000. You're a bounty hunter. You murdered my uncle for $5,000.
jokes.setup: Doug learns about the $5,000 loan arrangement
jokes.punchline: Escalates Larry from opportunist to professional assassin
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the murder accusation with the bounty hunter terminology. Makes Larry sound like a professional killer.

jokes.text: My neck. Let go of him!
jokes.setup: Doug puts Larry in headlock at funeral
jokes.punchline: Larry's neck injury gets worse from the fight
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the original neck problem, which gets worse from the confrontation. Sets up the return to acupuncturist.

jokes.text: Yeah, she asked me how I was feeling. I said, 'Better.' Better than before, not 100% better, not all better, but better.
jokes.setup: Acupuncturist claims Larry said he was better and owes money
jokes.punchline: Larry's semantic argument about degrees of 'better'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry semantic argument. The repetition of 'better' with different qualifiers shows his lawyerly precision and social awkwardness.

jokes.text: In English, 'better' doesn't mean better, completely better. I don't need a.... There's a confusion here, a language confusion.
jokes.setup: Continuing the semantic argument about 'better'
jokes.punchline: Larry's impossible explanation that 'better' doesn't mean better
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation where Larry ties himself in semantic knots. The absurdity of explaining that 'better' doesn't mean better is peak Larry.

jokes.text: Listen, Larry-San, this will cause me some great shame with my family. Everyone knows about this agreement. My employees, my business.
jokes.setup: Acupuncturist tries to guilt Larry into paying
jokes.punchline: The stakes keep escalating from money to family honor
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that adds cultural pressure to the financial pressure. The shame angle is well-played.

jokes.text: I have a check made out to me for $5,000. I can endorse it over to you. Don't cash it for a week.
jokes.setup: Larry needs to pay the acupuncturist $5,000
jokes.punchline: He uses Barry's check (from dead father) to pay the acupuncturist
jokes.explanation: Brilliant plot resolution that ties all storylines together. Larry uses blood money to pay medical bills, completing the circle perfectly.

jokes.text: Ancient Japanese custom.
jokes.setup: Larry asks if sending orchids is something acupuncturist does for all patients
jokes.punchline: Acupuncturist's deadpan claim it's an ancient custom
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to the orchid mystery. The deadpan delivery and obvious fabrication make it clear he's just covering for his personal gesture.

tmdb_overview: Larry promises to pay an acupuncturist $5,000 if he can cure his neck. Meanwhile, a struggling writer asks Larry for a $5,000 loan which he promises to pay back after his father dies.
